entry.title: Net2Net
entry.content:
Net2Net: Accelerating Learning via Knowledge Transfer

1. 什么是Net2Net

Net2Net，指利用知识迁移，去解决大型网络训练难度大、训练慢的问题。

先训练一个小网络，再利用Net2Net，训练一个更宽更深的网络，但是这个大网络并非train from scratch，我们将小网络中的权重进行变换以后，作为大网络的初始化权重，从而加速大网络的训练过程。这个网络权值再利用的过程，就是知识迁移。

现实世界中我们遇到的大部分机器学习系统，应该都是属于终身学习系统(Lifelong learning System)，再系统使用的过程中，也不断有新的数据加入，改善模型。刚开始的时候，数据量比较小，所以我们使用简单的网络，加快训练速度的同时，还能防止过拟合。但是数据量一步步增大的时候，简单模型可能不能够胜任需要，这个时候我们就需要拓展模型，将小网络加宽加深。

问题：如何在小网络的基础上面进行拓展，改变网络的拓扑结构以后，小网络的权重如何利用在大网络上面？

改变拓扑结构但是不改变网络的效果，对于同样的输入有同样的输出。这就是Net2Net的思想。

2. 如何进行Net2Net

定义两个操作Net2WiderNet，Net2DeeperNet

2.1 Net2WiderNet

Net2WiderNet 操作使得某一层更宽，例如让全连接层有更多的单元，让卷积层有更多的channel。
我们希望能够使得这层更宽并且变化后的结构对于同样的输入会得到相同的输出。

如上图，对于一个全连接层来说，如果我们新增了一个节点，那么我们随机从已有节点中选择一个节点copy它的输入权重，使得这个节点的值和已有选择的节点的值相同，对于输出的节点来说，需要把前一层的节点的值求和激活，这时我们发现我们选择的那个节点的值扩大了两倍，于是我们可以把他们各自都除以2，这样我们就实现了全连接层的恒等替换。

对于一个卷积层来说，道理也类似，如果我想增加一个channel，我可以随机选一个channel然后copy它的权重(filter)，对于输出时要再进行卷积的filter而言，我们把filter中这两层的channel的权重除以2就可以，这样也在channel增加的情况实现了恒等替换。

2.2 Net2DeeperNet

Net2DeeperNet操作使得我们可以增加一层，例如在一个全连接层后面再加一个全连接层，在一个卷积层后面再加一个卷积层。

对于一个全连接层来说，我们利用一个单位矩阵做权值，添加一个和上一个全连接层维度完全相同的全连接层，把前一个全连接层的值copy过来，实现恒等映射，此时再结合Net2WiderNet，就可以使这一层再变宽。但是我们可能很难实现把这个层变瘦。

对于一个卷积层来说，我们利用一个只有中间位置是1，其它位置全是0的filter，就很轻松的实现了恒等映射。

3. Summary

综上，我们通过定义两个操作，实现了从小网络到大网络的转换，但是我们这个不是一个可以泛化的方法，有一定的局限性，因为不是所有的情况都有事先恒等映射的方法。

但是对于全连接层和卷积层等来说，我们可以利用如上的方法大大加速大网络的训练，尤其是在结合终身机器学习系统或者神经网络架构搜索的方向上。

entry.title: A survey: Neural Architecture Search
entry.content:
[TOC]

1. Searching Method

Up to now, there mainly exsits three popular NAS frameworks.

  1. reinforcement learning
  2. evolutionary algorithms
  3. differentiable NAS

RL-based and EA-based NAS methods suffer from the problem that they require heavy computional resources and time, which is unacceptable for scientists. Thus, gradient-based NAS approches become more popular these days.

1.1 Reinforcement Learning

Zoph et al. use a RNN controller to generate the width, height or other facotrs of the CNN.

Zoph B, Le Q V. Neural architecture search with reinforcement learning

entry.title: NAP
entry.content:
NAP: Neural Architecture search with Pruning

In recent years, there has been continuously increasing attention attracted by Neural Architecture Search (NAS). Due to its computational efficiency, gradient-based NAS methods like DARTS has become the most popular framework for NAS tasks. Nevertheless, as the search iterates, the derived model becomes dominated by {\it skip-connect}s, causing the performance downfall. In this work, we present a novel approach to alleviate this issue, named Neural Architecture search with Pruning (NAP). Unlike prior differentiable architecture search works, our approach draws the idea from network pruning. We first train an over-parameterized network, including all candidate operations. Then we propose a criterion to prune the network. Based on a different relaxation of architecture representation, NAP can derive the most potential model by removing trivial and redundant edges from the whole network topology. Experiments show the effectiveness of our proposed approach. Specifically, the model searched by NAP achieves state-of-the-art performances (2.48% test error) on CIFAR-10. We transfer the model to ImageNet and obtains 25.1% test error with only 5.0M parameters, which is on par with modern NAS methods.

entry.title: DASH: Dominant-weighted neural Architecture SearcH
entry.content:
DASH: Dominant-weighted neural Architecture SearcH

Recently, there has been increasing attention attracted by neural architecture search (NAS). Traditional NAS approaches based on reinforcement learning or evolutionary algorithms which take thousand of GPU hours are strongly limited by the resources and time. In this work, we present a new gradient-based approach, namely Dominant-weighted neural Architecture SearcH (DASH). Unlike prior differentiable architecture search works, our DASH learns candidate operations individually and searches for the dominant-weighted edges as the most potential architecture. Experiments show the effectiveness of our proposed approach. Specifically, the model searched by DASH achieves a new state-of-the-art performance of a 2.47% test error in CIFAR-10, and can be transferred to ImageNet to obtain a test error of 25.1% with only 5.0M parameters, which is on par with recent methods. Our code is available .
